content.description:
Hobson's reactive rendering pipeline re-renders views when the items they display change. The system is registry-based with optional selector filtering — no global tracking context, no atom tracking, no closure capture.

## Architecture

The pipeline lives in [viewport-rendering](item://f1111111-0008-0000-0000-000000000000) and has two core pieces:

1. **RenderInstanceRegistry** — indexes every rendered instance by `itemId`, `viewId`, and `parentId`
2. **Event-driven scheduling** — listens for `item:updated` / `item:deleted` events and schedules re-renders

## Data Flow

```
item:updated event
  → registry.byItemId.get(changedId)
  → Set<instanceId>
  → selector check (if Hob view with selector)
  → _pendingReRenders (microtask batched)
  → _flushReRenders (serialized, async)
  → re-render each affected item
```

## RenderInstanceRegistry

Three index maps:

| Map | Key | Value | Purpose |
|-----|-----|-------|---------|
| `byItemId` | item GUID | Set of instance IDs | Which instances display this item? |
| `byViewId` | view GUID | Set of instance IDs | Re-render all instances using a specific view |
| `byParentId` | parent GUID | Set of instance IDs | Find children of a container |

Each instance stores: `domNode`, `itemId`, `viewId`, `parentId`, `decorator`, `openIn`, `ancestors`, `selector`, `timestamp`.

## Selector-Filtered Reactivity (Hob Views)

Hob views can declare which slice of an item's data they care about using `get-item` with a `:select` keyword:

```clojure
(get-item item-id :select (fn [item]
  (select-keys (get item :content) [:name :status])))
```

This registers a selector on the render instance. On subsequent `item:updated` events:

1. The selector function runs against the current item
2. The result is compared to the previous value via `deepEquals`
3. If unchanged → **skip re-render** (the key optimization)
4. If changed → update stored value, queue re-render

### Selector Pins

Once a selector is registered via `get-item :select` for an item, later bare `get-item` calls on the same item don't widen the selector. The selector is the intentional reactivity contract — it defines exactly what data matters.

### JS Views

JS views have no selector support. They are registered in `byItemId` and always re-render when their item changes.

## Batching and Serialization

Re-renders are **microtask batched**: multiple `item:updated` events in the same tick accumulate instance IDs in `_pendingReRenders`, then a single `_flushReRenders()` processes them all.

Flushes are **serialized**: if a flush is already in progress, new pending items wait. After each flush completes, a post-flush check picks up anything that arrived during the flush.

## Nested Rendering

Views can render child items via `render-item`. Each child gets its own render instance with independent selector tracking. Parents don't depend on children — editing a child re-renders only that child, not the parent.

## History

This system replaced the `DependencyTracker` (a stack-based tracking context that recorded item reads during view evaluation). The DependencyTracker was removed in Phase 7 simplification because:

- Global `_currentTrackingContext` state was fragile across async boundaries
- Atom tracking added complexity for minimal benefit
- `wrapEventHandler` context nesting was error-prone
- Registry-based lookups with explicit selectors are simpler and more predictable

See [Rendering Architecture Review](item://c0c0c0c0-0062-0000-0000-000000000000) (archived) for the analysis that led to this design.

## See Also

- [Views & Rendering](item://a0a0a0a0-d0c0-4000-8000-000000000004) — View system overview
- [viewport-rendering](item://f1111111-0008-0000-0000-000000000000) — Implementation
- [Silent Update Pattern](item://c0c0c0c0-0010-0000-0000-000000000000) — Two-tier save leveraging selectors
- [Stable Skeleton with Surgical Updates](item://c0c0c0c0-0017-0000-0000-000000000000) — Efficient local-state views
